school: The critical link between Ibrahim ibn Adham's ascetic tradition and the formal Chishti order; brought the Sufi path from Syria to Chisht (in modern Afghanistan) and trained Abu Ahmad Chishti, who gave the order its name; his transmission created the lineage through which Moinuddin Chishti would eventually take the path to India — making him the grandfather of the entire Indian Chishti tradition

Critical link in the Naqshbandi silsila between Bayazid Bistami and Yusuf Hamadani; known for his doctrine of universal spiritual hospitality — the inscription above his hospice door read: 'Whoever enters this house, give them bread; ask not about their faith'; his sayings were compiled in Nūr al-ʿUlūm and deeply influenced Rumi and other Persian mystics
bio: Persian Sufi mystic (963-1033) associated with the early development of the Naqshbandiyya tradition. He was known for his spiritual teachings and influenced the spiritual lineage of Central Asian Sufism.

school: Khorasan / Naqshbandi Lineage — Bridge figure between the Baghdad school and the Naqshbandiyya and Yeseviyya

The actual founder of the Suhrawardi Sufi school — his nephew Shihab al-Din al-Suhrawardi is more famous, but Abu Najib established the foundational framework; his Adab al-Muridin (Etiquette of the Seekers) was the first systematic manual for Sufi conduct and became the blueprint for subsequent Sufi orders; key link between the Baghdad Junayd school and the institutionalised Suhrawardiyya

Abdul Khaliq Ghujdawani (d. 1179), who formulated the eight foundational principles of the Naqshbandi path, including silent dhikr and 'awareness in every breath.' He established the Khwajagan lineage.
bio: Central Asian Sufi master (1103-1179) who was a founding figure of the Naqshbandiyya order, one of the most influential Sufi traditions. He established principles of spiritual practice that became central to the order.

school: Qadiriyya / Maghrebi Sufism — teacher of Ibn Arabi's masters; the most important Sufi of the Muslim West
quotes: Whoever truly seeks God finds that God was seeking him first.
The veil between you and God is not God — it is yourself.
Contentment is a treasure that never runs out.

school: Moroccan School / Shadhiliyya Founding — Murdered on his mountain; his student founded the Shadhiliyya
bio: A Moroccan Shadhili master and saint, Ibn Mashish was highly venerated for his spiritual authority and became an important ancestor figure in the Shadhili lineage.

school: The spiritual master of Abu'l-Hasan al-Shadhili and thus the grandfather-figure of the entire Shadhiliyya order; his Salat al-Mashishiyya (Prayer of Ibn Mashish) is one of the most widely recited devotional prayers in the Islamic world, used daily by Shadhili followers across five continents; a descendant of the Prophet through Idris I of Morocco
Akbarian — student of Ibn Arabi's circle; known for shamahid (witnessing beauty) as spiritual practice
bio: Persian Sufi philosopher (1164-1238) influenced by Ibn Arabi's teachings and associated with the Akbarian tradition. He was known for his mystical poetry and philosophical contributions to Islamic thought.

school: Founder of the Suhrawardiyya order in South Asia (c. 1170–1262), based in Multan. Unlike the Chishti emphasis on poverty, he accepted state patronage and land grants, using wealth to fund Sufi institutions. His approach created a durable model for organised Sufism across the Punjab.

Chishti Sufi saint of Delhi (1173–1235) who brought the Chishti order from Ajmer to the capital. A disciple of Moinuddin Chishti, he was renowned for his devotion to mystical audition (sama’). According to tradition, he died during an ecstatic listening session. His shrine in Mehrauli, Delhi, has been a centre of pilgrimage for eight centuries.
quotes: The heart that loves God has no room for pride.
Service to the poor is service to God — there is no shorter path.
The disciple who serves his master has already begun to serve God.
The heart that loves God has no room for pride.
Service to the poor is service to God — there is no shorter path.
The disciple who serves his master has already begun to serve God.

school: Syed Muhammad Usman Marwandi, known as Lal Shahbaz Qalandar (1177–1274), a Sufi saint of Sehwan, Sindh, whose shrine is one of the most visited in Pakistan. The ecstatic dhammal dance there is iconic.
bio: A revered Qalandari mystic saint venerated across South Asia, particularly in Sindh, Lal Shahbaz Qalandar is known for his devotional poetry and spiritual legacy.

school: Chishti Order (Punjab) — Teacher of Nizamuddin Auliya; his Punjabi verses enshrined in the Sikh Guru Granth Sahib

books.title: Shaloks of Baba Farid (Punjabi verses)
books.note: His Punjabi verses are in the Sikh scripture (Guru Granth Sahib) — the only Muslim saint so honoured.

quotes: Pride is the veil between the servant and God — lower it and the light enters.
The dervish who needs nothing is richer than the king who has everything.
Whoever serves the creation has served the Creator.
Pride is the veil between the servant and God — lower it and the light enters.
The dervish who needs nothing is richer than the king who has everything.
Whoever serves the creation has served the Creator.

school: Shams al-Din Muhammad of Tabriz (c. 1185–1248), the wandering dervish whose encounter with Rumi in Konya in 1244 triggered the most famous spiritual transformation in Islamic history. His provocative teaching shattered Rumi's scholarly composure and ignited the ecstatic poetry of the Divan-i Shams. He disappeared mysteriously.
quotes: Past and future veil God from our sight — burn both of them with fire.
The message behind the words is what transforms — not the words themselves.
Whoever sees only himself in the mirror has not yet learned to look.

school: Hamid al-Din Nagori (d. 1276), third-generation Chishti master based in Nagaur, Rajasthan. Famous for extreme asceticism, refusal of royal patronage, and strict vegetarianism — unusual among Sufis.
bio: An early master of the Chishti order in India, Hamid al-Din Nagori was instrumental in establishing Sufi practices and teachings in the Indian subcontinent.

school: Chishti — earliest Sufi poet to write in Hindi (Hindawi); bridge between Arabic Sufism and Indian vernacular literature
bio: A 13th-century Chishti saint and scholar of Nagaur, known for his Islamic jurisprudence and spiritual contributions to the development of the Chishti order.

school: One of the earliest Sufi missionaries in Bengal; established the Suhrawardiyya presence in eastern India decades before the Chishti order arrived; his khanqah at Pandua (near modern Malda) became the first major centre of Islamic learning in Bengal; credited with mass conversions through spiritual charisma; his tomb remains a major pilgrimage site
bio: A Suhrawardiyya mystic and influential spiritual master, Jalal al-Din Tabrizi was known for his role in propagating Sufi teachings in medieval Persia and Anatolia.

school: Cousin and principal khalifa of Baba Farid; founded the Sabiri branch of the Chishti order, known for its intense austerity and Jalali temperament contrasting with the Nizami branch's accessibility; his shrine at Kalyar Sharif near Roorkee remains one of the most visited Sufi shrines in India; his spiritual lineage continued through figures like Ashraf Jahangir Semnani
bio: Indian Sufi saint (1196-1291) associated with the Chishti order and a disciple of Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti. He was influential in spreading Islamic spirituality in South Asia.

school: Abu al-Abbas Ahmad al-Mursi (1219–1287), the direct student and successor of Abu al-Hasan al-Shadhili as head of the Shadhiliyya order. His mosque in Alexandria remains one of the city's most important landmarks.

Greatest Chishti saint of Delhi (1238–1325) whose spiritual influence shaped the culture and politics of the Delhi Sultanate. Teacher of the poet Amir Khusrow, he emphasised love, compassion, and service to all regardless of religion. His shrine in Delhi is one of the most visited in the Islamic world.
quotes: If there is a Paradise on this earth, it is here, it is here, it is here.
Love for God is the foundation — and everything else is built upon it.
The heart is the throne of God — do not let anyone other than God sit upon it.
Humility is the crown of the saints — whoever wears it has already arrived.
Serve all people regardless of their faith — God’s mercy is wider than any religion.
The heart is the throne of God — do not let anyone other than God sit upon it.
Humility is the crown of the saints — whoever wears it has already arrived.
Serve all people regardless of their faith — God’s mercy is wider than any religion.

school: Suhrawardiyya Order (Multan) — His mausoleum in Multan is a UNESCO-listed masterpiece of Tughlaq architecture
bio: A 13th-14th century Suhrawardiyya saint of Multan, one of the most venerated Sufi saints of South Asia, known for his spiritual authority and miracles.

school: Chishti Order (Delhi) — Student and successor of Nizamuddin Auliya; his death marked the end of the Delhi Chishti golden age
bio: A 14th-century Chishti saint of Delhi, successor to Nizam al-Din Auliya, instrumental in spreading the Chishti order throughout India.

school: Suhrawardiyya Order (Uch Sharif) — Descendant of Baha-ud-din Zakariya; travelled from Arabia to China
bio: A Suhrawardiyya saint and spiritual guide in medieval India, Makhdum Jahaniyan was influential in spreading Islamic mystical teachings in the Deccan region.

school: Amir Kulal (1278–1370), the pivotal teacher who transmitted the Khwajagan tradition directly to Baha al-Din Naqshband. A potter by trade in Bukhara, he combined craft labour with spiritual practice.
Kubrawiyya-Hamadaniyya — three times to Kashmir; converted the Kashmiri rulers to Islam; called 'Bab ul-Islam of Kashmir'
bio: A prominent Kubrawiyya mystic and scholar of the 14th century who played a significant role in spreading Islamic spirituality in Kashmir and Central Asia.

school: Chishti Order (Deccan) — Brought Chishti tradition to the Deccan; most prolific Chishti writer in Urdu and Persian
bio: A major figure of the Chishti order, Gesu Daraz was a renowned saint and spiritual master in the Deccan region of India with considerable influence on Indian Sufism.

school: Shadhiliyya — greatest Moroccan Sufi of the 14th century; his letters on spiritual trial shaped Islamic mystical psychology
bio: An Andalusian Shadhili mystic and scholar, Ibn Abbad al-Rundi made important contributions to Sufi doctrine and was known for his letters and spiritual guidance.

school: Shadhiliyya-Wafa'iyya — son of Muhammad Wafa; led the Egyptian Shadhili tradition; famous for mystical poetry
bio: Egyptian Sufi saint (1357-1405) associated with the Shadhiliyya order. He was known for his spiritual teachings and became an important figure in Egyptian Islamic mysticism.

school: Shadhiliyya-Jazuliyya — revived the Shadhili order in Morocco; wrote the Dala'il al-Khayrat, the most printed Islamic text after the Quran
bio: A 15th-century Moroccan Shadhiliyya saint and scholar, famous for composing the Dala'il al-Khayrat, one of the most widely read Islamic texts.

school: Naqshbandiyya — most powerful Naqshbandi master of the Timurid era; de facto ruler of Transoxiana through spiritual influence

The most politically influential Naqshbandi master; effectively the power behind the Timurid throne in Central Asia; his vast landholdings and network of followers made the Naqshbandiyya the dominant spiritual force from Transoxiana to Herat; his Faqarat is a key text of Naqshbandi spiritual practice; direct link between Baha-ud-Din Naqshband and Ahmad Sirhindi in the silsila
bio: A prominent Naqshbandiyya shaikh and political figure in Central Asia, Khwaja Ahrar was influential in both spiritual and temporal affairs of Timurid territories.

school: Bali Efendi of Sofia (d. 1553), the most important Naqshbandi shaykh in Ottoman Balkan history. He was the spiritual teacher of Grand Vizier Sokollu Mehmed Pasha and spread the Naqshbandiyya into Southeastern Europe.
bio: An Ottoman mystic and scholar of the Naqshbandiyya order who lived during the height of the Ottoman Empire and contributed to Sufi thought in Anatolia.

school: Shadhiliyya / Khalwatiyya — prolific Sufi author who reconciled Islamic law and mysticism in Ottoman Egypt
quotes: The Sufi path and the Sharia are one road — whoever separates them has left both.
God does not withhold from the sincere seeker — only the insincere one is left empty.
The measure of a man’s love for God is the measure of his service to God’s creation.
The Sufi path and the Sharia are one road — whoever separates them has left both.
God does not withhold from the sincere seeker.
The measure of a man’s love for God is the measure of his service to God’s creation.

school: Shattariyya — major Sufi master of Mughal India; introduced the Shattariyya order widely; teacher of Tansen (Akbar's musician)
bio: A prominent 16th-century Suhrawardiyya saint and scholar of Gwalior, influential in spreading Islamic mysticism in medieval India.

school: Bibi Jamal Khatun (d. c. 1647), daughter of the great Qadiri saint Mian Mir of Lahore and one of the most celebrated female Sufis of Mughal India. Her shrine in Lahore is visited by both men and women.

Abdal Khan Sarwari (d. c. 1610), a major Qadiri Sufi saint of Lahore whose shrine at Shah Jamal remains one of the most visited in the Punjab. The weekly dhammal gatherings at his shrine continue to this day.
bio: Indian Sufi mystic and spiritual master (1533-1605) associated with the Qadiriyya order. He was an influential teacher in the Indian subcontinent during the Mughal period.

school: Qadiriyya (Lahore) — Said to have laid the foundation stone of the Golden Temple of Amritsar; teacher of the Mughal prince Dara Shikoh
bio: A Qadiriyya saint and mystic of the 16th-17th century in the Punjab region, revered for his spiritual teachings and miracles.

school: Naqshbandiyya (India) — Brought the Mujaddidi branch of Naqshbandiyya to India; his student Ahmad Sirhindi became its greatest figure
bio: A pivotal Naqshbandiyya master who revived and reformed the order in India, Khwaja Baqi Billah was the teacher of Ahmad Sirhindi and a major figure in Indo-Islamic mysticism.

school: Khalwatiyya-Naqshbandiyya — greatest Sufi of the Makassar kingdom; exiled by the Dutch to Sri Lanka, then Cape Town where he spread Islam in South Africa
bio: A 17th-century Naqshbandiyya mystic from Makassar who became an influential Islamic scholar and spiritual leader in Southeast Asia.

school: Naqshbandiyya-Mujaddidiyya (Delhi) — Martyred; assassinated 1781 for his view that Hindu scriptures contained divine wisdom
bio: An 18th-century Naqshbandiyya saint and scholar based in Delhi, known for his spiritual leadership and Islamic scholarship during the Mughal period.

school: The primary successor of Mirza Mazhar Jan-e-Janan and one of the most influential Naqshbandi masters of 19th-century India; his Maqamat-i-Mazhari documents his teacher's spiritual stations; under his guidance the Delhi khanqah became the main centre transmitting the Mujaddidi branch of Naqshbandiyya; trained hundreds of khalifas who spread the order across India, the Arab world, and Southeast Asia
bio: Indian Naqshbandi Sufi master and saint who was influential in spreading the Naqshbandiyya order in South Asia during the 18th-19th centuries.

school: Influential reformist Sufi master whose students founded several major African orders; emphasised direct attachment to the Prophet (al-Tariqa al-Muhammadiyya) over affiliation to any particular silsila; his students founded the Sanusiyya (Muhammad al-Sanusi), the Khatmiyya (Muhammad Uthman al-Mirghani), and the Rashidiyya orders, reshaping Islam across Sudan, Libya, Somalia, and West Africa
quotes: The Prophet is the sun — we are the moons that reflect his light.
Purify the intention and you will find the action follows naturally.
Seek God in your every breath — for every breath that passes without remembrance is a loss.

school: Founded the Sanusiyya order in Cyrenaica (Libya) in 1837, establishing a network of zawiya lodges across the Sahara that served simultaneously as mosques, schools, law courts, and trading posts; his movement provided the organisational basis for Libyan resistance to Italian colonisation and later the Libyan state; combined Shadhili spirituality with Hanbali-influenced reform theology
quotes: The first obligation upon the Muslim is to know God — and to know God is to know His attributes.
Creed is the foundation — all acts of worship built on a cracked foundation will not stand.
The scholar who does not act on his knowledge is like a lamp that gives no light.

school: Algerian national hero, Qadiri Sufi master, and scholar (1808–1883) who led armed resistance against the French conquest of Algeria for fifteen years. After defeat and exile, he settled in Damascus where he saved thousands of Christians during the 1860 massacres. He was also a devoted student of Ibn Arabi’s philosophy.
bio: Algerian Islamic scholar, Sufi, and military leader (1808-1883) who led resistance against French colonization and became a symbol of Islamic spirituality and political resistance. He founded the Qadiriyya order and was recognized as a prominent Islamic reformer.
quotes: I am a slave of God and my face is turned toward God

school: Chishti-Sabiri — The paradoxical fact that both Gangohi (Deoband) and Ahmad Raza Khan (Barelvi) were his disciples makes him Islam's most ecumenical 19th-century figure
bio: An influential Chishti shaikh and Islamic reformer who initially taught in Mecca before establishing himself as a spiritual guide with many notable disciples in South Asia.

school: Rifaiyya / Qadiriyya — major Sufi master of Bengal; spread the Rifai and Qadiri orders widely in East India
bio: A 19th-century Suhrawardiyya saint and scholar, influential in promoting Islamic spirituality and reform in the Muslim world.

school: Qadiri and Chishti Sufi saint of Golra Sharif, near Islamabad (1859–1937). He was known for his scholarship, his defence of the Prophet’s honour, and his interfaith debates. His shrine remains one of the most visited in Pakistan, and he is revered for his miracles and his mastery of Persian and Arabic poetry.
bio: A Qadiriyya mystic and reformer in late colonial India, Meher Ali Shah was known for his spiritual teachings and efforts to revitalize Islamic practice among Muslims.

school: Shadhiliyya-Alawiyya — Swiss metaphysician who founded the Traditionalist/Perennialist school; initiated by Ahmad al-Alawi
bio: A Swiss-American metaphysicist and founder of the Shadhiliyya-influenced Maryamiyya order, Schuon was known for his writings on perennialism and comparative religion.
quotes: Beauty is the splendor of the true.

school: Abu Abdullah Muhammad al-Arabi al-Darqawi (Arabic: محمد العربي الدرقاوي; 1760–1823) was a Moroccan Sufi leader of the Shadhili tariqa and the author of letters concerning the dhikr he preached and instructions for daily life. He stressed noninvolvement in worldly affairs (Dunya) and spoke against other Sufi orders exploiting claims of barakah (blessings). He was imprisoned by the Moroccan ruler Mulay Slimane (r. 1792–1822) for supporting revolts against the throne, but was released by Abderrahmane (r. 1822–1859). A branch of the Shadhili order, the Darqawa, was organized around his teachings after his death, with members coming from a wide range of social groups. Though the Darqawa was once the most important tariqah in Morocco, its power waned as it spread throughout North Africa. His tomb is in the Zawiya Bou Brih also in the Rif.
bio: An 18th-19th century Shadhiliyya saint and reformer from Morocco, known for his emphasis on spiritual purification and Islamic renewal.

school: Abū Bakr 'Abdollāh b. Moḥammad b. Šahāvar b. Anūšervān al-Rāzī (Persian: نجم‌الدین رازی) commonly known by the laqab, or sobriquet, of Najm al-Dīn Dāya, meaning "wetnurse" (573 AH/1177 – 654 AH/1256) was a 13th-century Sufi. Hamid Algar, translator of the Persian Merṣād to English, states the application of "wetnurse" to the author of the Merṣād derives from the idea of the initiate on the Path being a newborn infant who needs suckling to survive. Dāya followed the Sufi order, Kubrawiyya, established by one of his greatest influences, Najm al-Dīn Kubrā. Dāya traveled to Kārazm and soon became a morīd (pupil, one who follows the shaykh master and learns from him, undergoing spiritual training) of Najm al-Dīn Kubrā. Kubrā then appointed Shaikh Majd al-Dīn Bagdādī as the spiritual trainer who also became Dāya's biggest influence. Dāya constantly refers to al-Dīn Bagdādī as "our shaikh." When his master, Najm al-Dīn Kubrā, was murdered in 618/1221, Dāya fled to Hamadan, then to Ardabil, and then to Anatolia where he finally settled with a fellow contemporary master Rumi.
bio: A 12th-13th century Kubrawiyya mystic from Khorasan, known for his spiritual teachings and philosophical contributions to Islamic Sufism.

school: Imam Hassan Ukhra Muhammad Azeem Barkhiya (1898 – 27 January 1979) (Urdu: امام حسن اخرٰی محمد عظیم برخیا), commonly referred to as Qalandar Baba Auliya (Urdu: قلندر بابا اولیاء), was the founding Imam and Spiritual Master of the Azeemiyya Sufi Order, which takes its name from him. He was a Scholar of Tassawuf, and is considered to be of the Abdāl, commonly being known by his honorific "Abdāl Haqq" (Urdu: ابدال حق). He was a student of Tajuddin Baba. He appointed Khawaja Shamsuddin Azeemi as head of the Silsila, and passed on 27 January 1979. Every January, his Urs (Death Anniversary) is conducted in which members of the Silsila congregate to recite the Quran and conduct gathering on matters of Tassawuf. His resting place is located in the Shadman district of Karachi, Pakistan.
bio: A 20th-century Pakistani Chishti saint based in Lahore, revered for his spiritual guidance and role in Islamic education and reform.

school: Sayyid Ali Hamadani was a Persian Sufi master of the Kubrawiyya order who played a pivotal role in the spread of Islam in Kashmir. He travelled with hundreds of disciples to the Kashmir valley, where he established mosques and khanqahs. His numerous works on Sufism, ethics, and governance earned him the title 'Shah-i Hamadan' (King of Hamadan).
bio: A 14th-century Kubrawiyya saint and missionary who was instrumental in establishing Islamic spirituality in Kashmir and surrounding regions.

school: Khwaja Usman Harooni was a major Sufi master of the Chishti order. He was the spiritual teacher of Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti, who would go on to establish the Chishti order in India. His emphasis on love, service, and spiritual poverty defined the character of the Chishti path for generations of followers.
bio: A 12th-13th century Chishti saint and early master of the Chishti order, instrumental in establishing its spiritual foundations in South Asia.

school: Yahya al-Shirvani al-Bakuvi was a Sufi master who founded the Khalwatiyya order, one of the most widespread Sufi tariqas in the Ottoman world. Based in Shirvan (modern Azerbaijan), he developed a systematic approach to spiritual retreat (khalwa) that became the hallmark of his order. The Khalwatiyya spread throughout Anatolia, the Balkans, Egypt, and North Africa.
bio: A 15th-century Khalwatiyya mystic from Shirvan who was influential in the development of Sufi mysticism in the Caucasus region.

school: Abd al-Razzaq al-Qashani was a Persian Sufi scholar and a leading expositor of Ibn Arabi's metaphysical system. His commentary on the Fusus al-Hikam and his Sufi lexicon 'Istilahat al-Sufiyya' became standard reference works in the Akbarian tradition. His mystical Quran commentary, long misattributed to Ibn Arabi himself, was widely circulated.
bio: Persian Sufi mystic and philosopher (1201-1330) influenced by Ibn Arabi's teachings and known for his mystical interpretations of Islamic theology. He was a prominent figure in the Akbarian tradition of Islamic mysticism.

school: Baba Farid, or Fariduddin Ganjshakar, was a major Chishti Sufi saint whose spiritual influence shaped the religious landscape of Punjab. His Punjabi verses are among the earliest literary works in the language and were later included in the Sikh scripture Guru Granth Sahib. His shrine at Pakpattan remains one of the most visited in South Asia.
bio: Indian Sufi saint (1175-1265) associated with the Chishti order and known as one of the great masters of the tradition. He was influential in establishing and spreading Chishti Sufism in South Asia.

school: Jahanara Begum was the eldest daughter of Shah Jahan and a significant Sufi author in the Qadiriyya order. She wrote 'Munis al-Arwah,' a biography of Chishti saints, and 'Risala-i Sahibiyya,' an account of her initiation into the Qadiri path. She wielded considerable political influence as Padshah Begum and was a major patron of architecture and Sufi institutions in Mughal Delhi.
bio: A Mughal princess and devoted follower of Qadiriyya Sufism, Jahanara Begum was a major patron of Sufi orders and wrote works on Islamic spirituality.

school: Mahmud Hudayi was a prominent Ottoman Sufi master of the Jelveti branch of the Khalwatiyya order. He served as spiritual advisor to several Ottoman sultans and authored numerous works on Sufi practice and theology in Turkish and Arabic. His tekke in Uskudar, Istanbul, remains an active centre of Sufi devotion.
bio: An Ottoman Khalwatiyya master and poet, Mahmud Hudayi founded an influential dervish order and contributed significantly to Ottoman Sufi literature and practice.

school: Sheikh Ahmadou Bamba was a Senegalese Sufi saint and the founder of the Muridiyya order, one of the largest Sufi brotherhoods in West Africa. His emphasis on work ethic, devotion, and peaceful resistance to French colonialism made him a revered figure. The city of Touba, built around his mosque, is the spiritual capital of the Muridiyya and hosts an annual pilgrimage of millions.
bio: A 19th-20th century Senegalese Islamic scholar and founder of the Muridiyya order, a major Sufi tradition in West Africa.

school: Ahmad al-Tijani was an Algerian Sufi master who founded the Tijaniyya order, one of the most widespread Sufi brotherhoods in Africa. After receiving a vision of Prophet Muhammad, he established his order in Fez, Morocco. The Tijaniyya spread rapidly across West and North Africa and today claims tens of millions of followers, particularly in Senegal, Nigeria, and the Maghreb.

Ahmad al-Rifa'i was an Iraqi Sufi saint and the founder of the Rifa'iyya order, one of the oldest Sufi tariqas. Based in the marshlands of southern Iraq, he emphasised humility, service to the poor, and ecstatic devotional practices. The Rifa'iyya became one of the most widespread Sufi orders in the Arab world, Turkey, and the Balkans.
bio: Iraqi Sufi mystic (1118-1182) who founded the Rifa'iyya order, one of the major Islamic Sufi traditions. He was known for his spiritual teachings and ecstatic mystical practices.

school: Zahed Gilani was a Persian Sufi master who founded the Zahediyya order in Gilan. He was the spiritual teacher of Safi al-Din Ardabili, whose descendants would go on to establish the Safavid dynasty and transform Iran. The Zahediyya-Safaviyya lineage represents one of the most consequential Sufi-political connections in Islamic history.
bio: A 13th-century Persian mystic and founder of the Zahediyya Sufi order, known for his ascetic practices and spiritual teachings.
